coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial activities of microwave-assisted synthesized polypyrrole/chitosan and poly (pyrrole-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine) stimulated by C-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Maruthapandi, Moorthy
Sharma, Kusha
Luong, John H.T.
Gedanken, Aharon
coredata.dc:description: Polypyrrole grafted with chitosan (PPy-g-CS) and poly (pyrrole-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine, a copolymer, (COP) have been synthesized by a one-step microwave procedure with carbon dots(C-Dots) as initiators. The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged polymers and negatively charged microbial cell membranes is widely anticipated to be responsible for cellular lysis. However, Escherichia coli exposed to PPy-g-CS (zeta potential = +46.9 mV) was completely perished after 3 h while COP (zeta potential = +64.1 mV) exhibited no antimicrobial effect. The two polymers were capable of eradicating Staphylococcus aureus, implying the charged effect is the main mechanism of cell death. The two polymers could also chelate calcium and other nutrients as well as form an external barrier to suppress the penetration of essential nutrients to support microbial survival and proliferation. In particular, pyrrole grafted chitosan was reasoned to stack onto the bacterial surface to impede the mass transfer and suppress the bacterial metabolic activity. The binding of chitosan to teichoic acids, essential acids of Gram-positive bacteria, would provoke a sequence of events and lead to bacterial death.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial activities of microwave-assisted synthesized polypyrrole/chitosan and poly (pyrrole-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine) stimulated by C-dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polypyrrole grafted with chitosan (PPy-g-CS) and poly (pyrrole-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine, a copolymer, (COP) have been synthesized by a one-step microwave procedure with carbon dots(C-Dots) as initiators. The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged polymers and negatively charged microbial cell membranes is widely anticipated to be responsible for cellular lysis. However, Escherichia coli exposed to PPy-g-CS (zeta potential = +46.9 mV) was completely perished after 3 h while COP (zeta potential = +64.1 mV) exhibited no antimicrobial effect. The two polymers were capable of eradicating Staphylococcus aureus, implying the charged effect is the main mechanism of cell death. The two polymers could also chelate calcium and other nutrients as well as form an external barrier to suppress the penetration of essential nutrients to support microbial survival and proliferation. In particular, pyrrole grafted chitosan was reasoned to stack onto the bacterial surface to impede the mass transfer and suppress the bacterial metabolic activity. The binding of chitosan to teichoic acids, essential acids of Gram-positive bacteria, would provoke a sequence of events and lead to bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:para: As antibiotic resistance has become one of the serious problems facing humanity, the search for new effective antimicrobials with low cytotoxicity from natural sources has received considerable attention. Chitosan (CS), isolated from shrimps, is a versatile hydrophilic polysaccharide consisting of β- (1-4)- linked 2-amino-2-deoxy-D-glucose residues with a broad antimicrobial spectrum to various bacteria (Gram-negative and Gram-positive) and fungi (Cabuk, Yavuz, Ibrahim Unal, & Alan, 2015). Commercial chitosan (100-1000 kDa) with over 75-85% deacetylated (DA) units is also available. The minimum inhibition concentration (MIC) of chitosan is microorganism-dependent and very high, ranging from 10 to 5000 mg/L (ppm) (Goy, Britto, & Assis, 2009). As a weak base with a semi-crystalline structure, chitosan is practically insoluble in water. In acidic conditions below its pKa of ∼6.3, the amino groups of chitosan are partially protonated, resulting in the repulsion between positively charged macro chains to weaken both inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the diffusion of water molecules becomes easier, leading to the subsequent solvation of these macromolecules (Sogias, Khutoryanskiy, & Williams, 2010) Apparently, a combination of high MIC and low aqueous solubility at neutral pH are two major drawbacks for broader applications of chitosan in food and medical applications, as the pH plays an important role in its biocompatibility (Kurita, 2006).

The amino group in chitosan can be conjugated with the amino or carboxyl group of biomolecules or biopolymers using a crosslinking agent, e.g., glutaraldehyde for amino-amino crosslinking (Migneault, Dartiguenave, Bertrand, & Waldron, 2004) or carbodiimide activation (Williams & Ibrahim, 1981) for amino-carboxyl coupling. Worth noting is the grafting of polymers onto the chitosan backbone to improve the latter’s solubility and antimicrobial activity (Ignatova, Manolova, & Rashkov, 2007). In this context, pyrrole (Py) has received considerable attention as it can be polymerized by chemical (Tan & Ghandi, 2013) or electrochemical procedures (Sadki, Schottland, Brodie, & Sabouraud, 2000). The antimicrobial activity of the polypyrrole (PPy) grafted chitosan (PPy-g-CS) by chemical oxidative radical-polymerization synthesized is significantly enhanced compared to PPy and CS per se (Cabuk et al., 2015). The PPy/ZnO/CS (polypyrrole-zinc oxide-chitosan) also confers an enhanced antimicrobial effect on Pseudomonas aeruginosa with a low MIC (2-5 mg/mL) of PPy/ZnO/CS (Ebrahimiasl, Zakaria, Kassim, & Basri, 2015) and PPy/dextrin (Zare, Lakouraj, & Mohseni, 2014). To date, various polymeric composites have been reported and tested for their antimicrobial activities (Table 1 ). There is also considerable interest in pyrrole derivatives as antimicrobial agents against S. aureus, Bacillus subtilis, E. coli, and Candida albicans. The elapsed time to reach a complete mortality state for bacterial colonies is still 12 h, which is the best minimum time achieved by the hybrid CS/PANI (polyaniline)-PPy (Mohamed, El-Domany, & El-Hameed, 2009).

Besides electrostatic interactions between the polymers and bacterial cells, we hypothesize that other mechanisms also play a significant contribution to cell death. In particular, the hydrophobic part of the non-protonated amine groups of chitosan at neutral pH might be able to diffuse into the hydrophobic core of the cell membrane to provoke cell death. We also suggest a synergic effect arising from CS and PPy as the former competes with Ca2+, potassium, and phosphate, which are required as the energy source for bacteria whereas the latter confers a strong positive charge. In this context, two positively charged polymers are prepared: PPy grafted chitosan (PPy-g-CS) and poly (Py-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine) (COP), using a simple microwave-assisted polymerization process (Maruthapandi, Kumar, & Gedanken, 2018; Moorthy, Kumar, Porat, & Gedanken, 2018; Maruthapandi, Kumar, Levine, & Gedanken, 2018). This facile procedure enables the synthesis of the PPy-g-CS and COP, respectively, using carbon dots as the initiators. The two polymers are characterized by X-rays diffraction, FTIR, 13C-NMR, SEM, and zeta-potentials. The antimicrobial activities of the two newly synthesized polymers are then evaluated against one Gram-negative bacterium, E. coli and one Gram-positive bacterium, S. aureus as the test models. S. aureus is most dangerous amongst many common staphylococcal bacteria, which cause pneumonia, heart valve infections, and bone infections. E. coli can cause intestinal infection by coming into contact with the feces, or stool, of human or animals. A plausible mechanism for the microbial activity of the two polymers is then postulated in corroboration with pertinent experimental data.

Citric acid, D- L-cysteine, chitosan (CS, MW. 50 - 190 kDa, degree of deacetylation or DDA = 75%), pyrrole (Py), hydrochloric acid, N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine (NED) and sulfuric acid were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich.

C-dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal method by admixing 0.6 g citric acid and 0.4 g of D, L-cysteine in 60 mL of doubly distilled water for 10 min at room temperature. The resulting homogenous solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a hot air oven. The yellow color supernatant solution was collected after centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min (Moorthy et al., 2018).

0.9 mL of Py (15 mmol) and 5 mL of the C-dot solution were added to a flask containing 30 mL of HCl and H2SO4 (0.5 M, 1:1 molar ratio). After microwave irradiation for 2 min, the formation of a dark brown precipitate was observed. The precipitated material was collected by filtration, washed several times with distilled water, and dried at room temperature (Moorthy et al., 2018).

0.33 g of chitosan (CS) was dissolved in 30 mL of a 1:1 ratio of HCl and H2SO4 (0.5 M) aqueous solution with stirring to attain a homogeneous solution. After adding 15 mmol of pyrrole (Py) and 5 mL of the C-dot solution, the resulting solution was subject to microwave irradiation until the formation of a dark brown precipitate. The solid precipitate was separated by filtration, washed several times with distilled water, and dried at room temperature. The same procedure was followed for the synthesis of COP (0.75 mmol of Py and 2.7 mmol of NED).

The FTIR of the polymers was acquired by a Transon 27 instrument (Bruker, Germany), equipped with a diamond tip whereas UV-vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Cary Vin spectrophotometer. The solid-state properties of the amorphous synthesized polymers were detected with an AXS D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker) with a reflection of θ-geometry. The analysis was performed at 40 kV and 30 mA using Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) as the radiation source, a receiving slit width of 0.2 mm, and a high-resolution energy-dispersive detector. All MAS NMR experiments were carried out on a Bruker Advance III 5000 MHz narrow-bore spectrometer, using a 4 mm double-resonance MAS probe. 13C CPMAS was performed at a spinning rate of 8 kHz, using a 2.5 μs 1H 90° pulse, 2 ms mixing time, and a 3 s recycle delay between acquisitions. NMR spectra were acquired using a Bruker 5000 Ultra Shield spectrometer with 400 MHz NMR (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). The polymer thermal behavior was measured using a model Pyris 1 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Perkin Elmer). The analysis used dried ∼ 8 mg samples in a dynamic nitrogen atmosphere of 100 mL/min with a temperature range of 30 °C – 900 °C, and a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Zeta potential measurements of the polymers were performed by a ZetaSizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK). The samples were prepared by dispersing the polymers in deionized water (2 mg/10 mL) with sonication for 8 min at 25 °C.

The polymers were dispersed in distilled water (10 mg/10 mL) to test for their antibacterial activities against E. coli and S. aureus. The microorganisms were incubated overnight under aerobic conditions at 37 °C in a Luria-Bertani (LB) broth. The bacterial growth was measured using Abs595 (absorbance at 595 nm) until a final bacterial concentration of 107 cells was attained. For the antibacterial tests, 500 μL of the polymer sample was added to the 500 μL of the bacterial suspension. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm and 100 μL aliquots were removed after 0, 3, 6, and 24 h. The aliquot was diluted 10-fold in the 20% LB medium and plated on the nutrient agar plates. The plates were dried and incubated for 20 h at 37 °C. The bacterial colonies at the appropriate dilutions were counted and the number of viable bacteria was calculated using the CFU method.

The most-agreeable mechanism of pyrrole polymerization involves the oxidation of a pyrrole monomer yielding a radical cation (Chougule et al., 2011). A bipyrrole (dimer) is formed from the coupling of the two generated radical cations with deprotonation (Partch, Gangolli, Matijević, Cal, & Arajs, 1991). The bipyrrole (dimer) is oxidized again and couples with another oxidized segment. The propagation continues, enabling the polymer chains to grow and lead to the pyrrole polymer (PPy).

The X-Ray diffraction for PPy displayed three broad peaks associated with the scattering from PPy chains at the interplanar spacing are centered at 2θ∼, 18° (very intense and broad), 31.7° and 43.1° (Fig. 1 a). PPy synthesized using monomeric pyrrole with ammonium persulfate as an oxidizing agent shows only one single peak at about 2θ = 24˚ (Chougule et al., 2011). Such a single broad peak is characteristic of amorphous PPy (Partch et al., 1991) reflecting the scattering from PPy chains at the interplanar spacing (Ouyang & Li, 1997). Besides the amorphous nature of this polymer, the broad peak observed in the region of 2θ = 18-20˚ also reflects intermolecular stacking, in agreement with the literature (Chitte, Bhat, Walunj, & Shinde, 2011).

The COP also exhibited only a broad peak around 2θ = 16.4°- 23.9°, which could be ascribed to the repeated units of NED in the copolymer. This broad peak feature illustrated that COP is also amorphous in nature. As reported, the number and intensity of the XRD peaks of chitosan are dependent upon the degree of deacetylation (DDA) (Feng, Liu, Zhao, & Hu, 2012). The chitosan with 91% DDA showed two strong reflections at 2θ = 10.6˚ (peak 002) and 2θ = 20.4˚ (peak 110) whereas the chitosan with 52% DDA is reported to have a diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.1˚. In general, chitosan contains such semi-crystalline peaks due to the strong hydrogen bonding between molecules because of the free amino groups and hydroxyl groups. The XRD spectrum of PPy-g-CS prepared in this work displayed two sharp diffracted peaks around at 2θ = 10.6° and 20.4°, a typical signature of chitosan whereas the peak around 2θ = 43.1° reflected the presence of PPy together with CS. Apparently, the CS is incorporated in the PPy polymer still preserved its semicrystallinity.

The thermal stability of PPy, COP, and PPy-g-CS, respectively, was analyzed using thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. 1b). PPy and COP only lose 6% of their mass at 90 °C due to the evaporation of water with markedly higher thermal stability compared to PPy-g-CS. The TGA weight loss of PPy-g-CS also revealed two diverse weight loss stages: 14% of an initial weight loss at 215-310° C due to the thermal decomposition of oligomers and the second weight loss above 520 °C due to the thermal decomposition of the polymer. For COP, the first weight loss of 17% in the temperature range of 178-343 °C was attributed to the decomposition of COP. The second weight loss of 9 % in the temperature range 380-453 °C indicated the oligomer decomposition, followed by the third weight loss above 500 °C, an indicator of the total decomposition of this polymer. PPy exhibited a thermal profile, which was similar to that of COP. Considering the thermal stability of PPy-g-CS or COP up to 200 °C, the polymers are simply autoclaved together with the culture medium to prevent contamination. This is an important feature, enabling the manufacture of novel antimicrobials with high sterility.

The FTIR spectra of the PPy, COP, and PPy-g-CS materials are shown in Fig. 2 . The PPy has a few broad peaks around 1042-1704 cm-1, broadband at 3390 cm-1 owing to N-H stretching vibrations of pyrrole rings, and the peaks at 1596 and 1518 cm-1 related to C = C stretching. The peaks at 2981 and 2905 cm-1 correspond to the C-H aromatic stretching: the peak at 1334 cm-1 is assigned to the C-N stretching and the peaks around 1105 (very pronounced) and 1260 cm-1 (relatively weaker) reflect the C-C stretching and the = C-H in-plane vibrations, respectively. Some key peaks observed in this study agreed well with the literature information (Yussuf, Al-Saleh, Al-Enezi, & Abraham, 2018), confirming the formation of PPy using C-dots as the initiators instead of a chemical oxidizer, e.g., FeCl3 or ammonium persulfate. The PPy can also be prepared by electropolymerization (Sayyah, Mohamed, & Shaban, 2014; Yalçınkaya, Demetgül, Timur, & Çolak, 2010) and conjugated with chitosan to form PPy-g-CS (Rabea, Badawy, Stevens, Smagghe, & Steurbaut, 2003; Raymond, Morin, & Marchessault, 1993). The FTIR spectrum of the composite (PPy-g-CS) showed some combined features of PPy and CS, notable bands due to the CS-NHAc (acetyl) units (with C-O stretching) at 1655 cm-1. The spectrum also exhibited some features of pure chitosan: N–H bending at 1579 cm-1, C–N stretching coupled with N–H plane deformation at 1420 cm-1, symmetrical angular deformation of CH3 at 1376 cm-1, C–N stretching of the amino groups of at 1320 cm-1, C–O–C stretching vibration at 1030 cm-1, and the specific bands of the ®(1–4) glycoside bridge at 1154 and 896 cm-1. Surprisingly, COP exhibited a profile, which was very similar to that of PPy except some peaks were more intensified and sharper. The FTIR spectra for NED and Cs are provided in Fig S.1 and the corresponding peaks value are shown in Table 1 Supplementary Material (SI).

The polymers were also confirmed by solid-state 13C NMR spectroscopy, with the resultant spectra shown in Fig. 3 a. The solid-state NMR spectrum of PPy displays broad and sharp peaks. The peak at 125 ppm is assigned to the protonated C-1, C-10, C-11 and C-14 of the carbon in the polymer chain. The sharp peaks about 59 and 71 ppm are stemmed from the protonated C-9 and non-protonated carbon C-16 of the quinoid ring of the PPy structure. The shoulder peaks at 108 and 115 ppm are attributed to the protonated carbons of C-2, C-3, C-6 and C-7 in the aromatic rings, and the peak about 122 ppm corresponds to the non-protonated carbons of C-12 and C-13. 13C NMR spectra of chitin/chitosan in solid state have been reported from 0 to 250 ppm (Tripathi, Mehrotra, & Dutta, 2008).

The spectra of PPy-g-CS exhibited some combined features of PPy and CS, notably C1 at ∼100 ppm. No peaks at 180 ppm (C = O) and 20 ppm (CH3) were recorded and such results were expected as the chitosan used in this study has a very high degree of deacetylation (DDA = 75%), i.e., the removal of acetyl groups -COCH3 from the chitin original structure. In brief, there are 8 signals in the 13C NMR spectrum for chitin but only 6 for chitosan with a very high DDA. The C-1 atom for both chitin and chitosan molecules should resonate around 100 ppm and other carbon atoms resonate between 50 and 80 ppm. There was a doublet at 30-40 ppm, which could not be assigned considering the chitosan used in this study has a very high degree of deacetylation. Albeit a small broad peak at 180 ppm (C = O) was not observed as expected, the CH3 of chitosan if any should be positioned at 22-25 ppm. As expected, the COP composite displayed some key features of PPy and NED (Fig. 3a) as the latter has 5 peaks located from 122-129 ppm [https://spectrabase.com/spectrum/BgzRPsXWTe3]. In comparison to PPy, two new peaks in the COP could be ascertained the presence of NED in this copolymer. One might anticipate some significant peak shifts or peak merging when NED was copolymerized with pyrrole to form this copolymer, which exhibited one intensified peak with a left shoulder at 30-40 ppm. The chemical structures of PPy-g-CS and COP are described in Fig. 3b.

Morphology studies of the PPy, PPy-g-CS, and COP polymers were conducted by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 4 shows the SEM images of the PPy, PPy-g-CS, and COP polymers. The SEM image of the PPy displays agglomerated spherical micro-nano particles with a range of diameters up to 5 μm. In contrast, the electropolymerized PPy film consists of non-ideal spherical aggregates with the diameter ranging from 10 to 50 nm, however, the film also has an amorphous structure (Rabea et al., 2003).

Representative SEM micrographs of the PPy/chitosan micro-nano polymer surface are depicted in Fig. 4(b–c). These images indicate that the PPy-g-CS micro-nano polymer features an irregular spherical structure. The micro-nanoscale PPy particles were irregular in shape and ranged from 200-1000 nm in size and the layered structure. In general, PPy particles were aggregated on the chitosan layer. Fig. 4d shows that the COP is in the form of irregular spherical with flat-shaped particles in the range of 5 μm.

Antibiotics, small molecules with molecular weights of a few hundred Dalton, have been used to eradicate both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria with known mechanisms. Rifampin (MW 822.94 g/mol) is bactericidal and acts on both intracellular and extracellular organisms to suppress the initial RNA synthesis. Various antibiotics are effective against E. coli and chloramphenicol (MW 323.13 g/mol) acts by interfering with bacterial protein synthesis. The minimal inhibition concentration (MIC) of these two antibiotics is just a few μL/mL or lower, compared to over 23 μL/mL against S. aureus (Raafat, von Bargen, Haas & Sahl, 2008). However, antibiotic resistance is the impetus for the development of novel antibiotics from natural products and chitosan is one of the popular compounds.

Chitosan exhibits antimicrobial activities against a wide range of microorganisms because of ionic interactions with bacterial wall constituents (negative charge) due to its positively charged amino group (De Britto & Assis, 2007; Tripathi et al., 2008). The interaction event triggers the peptidoglycan hydrolysis in the microorganism wall to provoke the leakage of intracellular electrolytes and kill the microorganism. Thus, as higher the degree of deacetylation, higher will be the antimicrobial effectiveness (Jia & Xu, 2001; Rabea et al., 2003). Two other important points are the effect of the polymer molecular weight (MW) and the size of coiled of chitosan chains on bacterial growth and multiplication (Goy et al., 2009; Zheng & Zhu, 2003). The effect of MW is pH-dependent; however, the MW of chitosan should be higher than 10 kDa to exhibit antibacterial activity (Chang, Lin, Wu, & Tsai, 2015). Such results are not completely unexpected since the aforementioned parameters are related to the zeta potential of chitosan (Jeon & Kim, 2000). This was the rationale behind the use of chitosan with a degree of deacetylation of 75 % with molecular weights spanning 50 - 190 kDa in this work.

At pH 7, chitosan (pKa∼ 6.3) has a slight positive charge of +0.78 mV and becomes less water-soluble, so it should not have strong interactions with the negatively charged microbial cell membranes to induce cell lysis as the main antimicrobial mechanism (Goy et al., 2009; Tripathi et al., 2008). Consequently, chitosan is subject to a quaternization step to methylate the amino group to trimethyl chitosan, RN(CH3)3+ (De Britto, Celi Goy, Campana Filho, & Assis, 2011). Trimethyl chitosan is a cationic polymer, which is independent of the solvent pH and displays a strong electrostatic interaction between its positively charged RN(CH3)3+ sites and negatively charged microbial cell membranes. Charged chitosan or trimethyl chitosan also interacts with essential nutrients required for the microbial growth (Jia & Xu, 2001). This antimicrobial mechanism should be anticipated for the grafting of chitosan to the PPy backbone with a positive charge of +41.8 mV. Indeed, the zeta potential at pH 7 of PPy-g-CS and COP was measured to be +46.9 mV and +64.1 mV, respectively.

In this study, E. coli and S. aureus were used for the antimicrobial assessment of the synthesized polymer materials using the CFU per mL method. CS itself has an antimicrobial activity due to the presence of its amino groups. At neutral pH, slightly positive charged chitosan (+0.78 mV) exhibited no antimicrobial effect against E. coli compared to PPy. However, PPy-g-CS completely suppressed the growth of E. coli in corroboration with an SEM micrograph obtained for this affected bacterium (Fig. 5 ). There was a delay in the effect of COP as the growth of E. coli was sustained for 3 after their exposure. Nevertheless, the antibacterial effect of this polymer was minimal against E. coli, compared to PPy-g-CS (Fig. 5a). The effects of PPy and CS against E. coli can be found in Fig. S2 (Supplementary Material). The bacterial cells were aggregated as shown in several SEM micrographs, in agreement with the observation of Raafat, von Bargen, Hass, and Sahl, (2008)). Thus, the positive charge of the polymers might be a fundamental factor contributing to their interaction with the negatively charged microbial cell surface, leading to impairment of vital bacterial activities (Je & Kim, 2006; Zakrzewska, Boorsma, Brul, Hellingwerf, & Klis, 2005).

Similar behavior was observed for the growth of S. aureus in the presence of PPy or CS. Again, CS exhibited no antimicrobial effect against this bacterium whereas PPy was somewhat effective, particularly after 24 h of incubation (Fig. S3, SI). In contrast, S. aureus was also eradicated by PPy-g-CS after 1 h, whereas COP remained ineffective for a duration of 6 h. Nevertheless, COP shows complete bacterial mortality after 24 h. (Fig. 6 ). The antimicrobial effect of COP against S. aureus (Gram-positive) but not E. coli (Gram-negative) was somewhat unexpected since the cell wall of E. coli has a thickness of 7–8 nm compared to 20-80 nm for that of S. aureus (Gram-positive), (Da Silva, de Britto, Seleghim, & Assis, 2010), i.e., the Gram-positive cells are more resistant to the cellular lysis. An SEM micrograph obtained for S. aureus cells treated with COP after 3 h into the experiment confirmed that the bacterial growth persisted for 6 h, compared to 3 h for E. coli (Fig. S4, Supplementary Material). The effects of PPy and CS against S. aureus can be found in Fig. S3 (Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: A postulated mechanism of cell death
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of PPy-g-Cs and COP has been inspired by the antimicrobial effect of natural cationic peptides with two key properties against bacterial growth (Gibney et al., 2012). First, the cationic groups of peptides enable electrostatic interactions of peptides to bacterial cell surfaces with a high negative charge. The peptide amphiphilic structure promotes the insertion of their hydrophobic side chains into the lipid membrane to disrupt the membrane integrity, resulting in the leakage of cellular components, and the breakdown of membrane potential. A combination of cationic and hydrophobic features in a polymer enhances the stability of hydrophobic chains against massive aggregation. To be effective, the hydrophobic part must diffuse into the hydrophobic core of the cell membrane to break the cellular structure (Klibanov, 2007). Guided by this antimicrobial mechanism, two positively charged polymers were synthesized: PPy-g-CS and COP and investigated for their antimicrobial effects against two model bacteria.

This work unraveled that the charged effect was not the only mechanism that suppressed the growth of E. coli because PPy-g-Cs (+46.9 mV) was more effective than COP (+64.1 mV). Doubtlessly, electrostatic contributions play an important role but there are other contributions to cell death. It was reasoned that the charge of PPy-g-Cs was sufficient to display electrostatic interactions with the bacterial membrane. The hydrophobic part of this polymer consisting of the non-protonated amine groups and the ring backbone of PPy might be able to diffuse into the hydrophobic core of the cell membrane to provoke cell death. COP is more structurally rigid and its hydrophobic interaction with the cell hydrophobic core is less effective compared to PPy-g-Cs. As described earlier, there was a delay of 3 h before a modest antimicrobial effect of COP was observed. The antimicrobial effect of COP is not known and requires further investigation. However, its analog, poly (ethylene mine, PEI), has been reported as an antimicrobial agent with selective activity (Gibney et al., 2012). This polymer has serves as drug carriers in biomedical applications because of its ability to enter cells or permeabilize cell membranes. The protonated ammonium groups of the PEI bind the negatively charged cell membrane whereas its non-protonated amine groups and ethylene backbone serve as hydrophobic groups. This hydrophobic domain can diffuse in the bacterial hydrophobic core to induce membrane disruption or permeabilization, leading to antibacterial activity (Gibney et al., 2012; Klibanov, 2007). N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine (NED) can form a complex with uranyl ion, UO2(NO3)2, and exhibit some moderate inhibitory activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, E. coli, Bacillus subtilis and S. aureus in comparison to tetracycline 9 (Alhadhrami, 2018). The effect of teichoic acids on the susceptibility of S. aureus to chitosan deserves a brief comment here as these essential polyanionic polymers of the cell walls appear to extend to the peptidoglycan layer surface. The binding of chitosan to teichoic acids, coupled with a potential extraction of membrane lipids (predominantly lipoteichoic acid) would provoke a sequence of events and lead to bacterial death. This postulated mechanism was supported by relevant observations as some S. aureus mutants with the lack of teichoic acids are more resistant to the microbial activity of chitosan (Raafat et al., 2008).

Another point of consideration is the meal chelation of PPy-g-CS. Besides excellent and well-known metal-binding capacities of the amine groups of CS, polypyrrole-based adsorbents can remove heavy metal ions from aqueous solutions (Mahmud, Obidul-Huq, & Yahya, 2016). Thus, PPy-g-CS prevents the influx of metals and essential nutrients to support bacterial growth (Chung, Wang, Chen, & Li, 2003; Cuero, Osuji, & Washington, 1991; Roller & Covill, 1999; Sudarshan, Hoover, & Knorr, 1992; Tokura, Ueno, Miyazaki, & Nishi, 1996; Vishu Kumar, Varadaraj, Gowda, & Tharanathan, 2005). In particular, PPy-g-CS competes with Ca2+ for the negative sites on the membrane surface (Young & Kauss, 1983). Calcium, potassium, and phosphate are required as the energy source and calcium efflux systems have been described in several bacteria (Rosch, Sublett, Gao, Wang, & Tuomanen, 2008). However, this route alone is not a determinant antimicrobial action considering the limited sites available of PPy-g-CS for such interactions.

Chitosan might pass the plasma membrane and interacts with microbial DNA, resulting in the inhibition of the mRNA and protein synthesis (Hadwiger, Kendra, Fristensky, & Wagoner, 1981; Sebti, Martial‐Gros, Carnet‐Pantiez, Grelier, & Coma, 2005; Sudarshan et al., 1992). Indeed, the presence of chitosan oligomers inside E. coli exposed to chitosan has been confirmed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Fei Liu, Lin Guan, Zhi Yang, Li, & De Yao, 2001). This expectation could be plausible considering chitosan as a linear polymer with a diameter of ∼ 1.1 nm in its extended conformation (Didenko et al., 2005), whereas bacterial pore sizes range from 2.06 - 3 nm (Vollmer & Holtje, 2004) and up to 7 nm (Fisher, Meroueh, & Mobashery, 2006). However, the probability of this event could be rather low, considering a large dimension of PPy-g-CS. chitosan. Chitosan alone probably exists in solution in a hydrated form with a hydrodynamic radius of 24.59 nm ± 1.4 nm (Raafat et al., 2008). Therefore, it is very unlikely PPy-g-CS with a size higher than this hydrodynamic radius can cross the cell wall. It is still unknown whether the chitosan of PPy-g-CS can be broken down by bacterial enzymes, particularly staphylococcal enzymes (Raafat et al., 2008), which might pass the cell wall. However, a rough estimation of the molecular weight of excluded compounds from E. coli is about 600 to 700 Dalton (Nakae, 1986).

Another possibility is the formation of an external barrier by PPy-g-CS to suppress the penetration of essential nutrients to support microbial growth. Chitosan can be stacked onto the bacterial surface to impede the mass transfer and suppress the bacterial metabolic activity (Vishu Kumar et al., 2005). In this context, the specific binding of a chitosan derivative to a receptor on the cell surface of P. aeruginosa has been reported (Morimoto et al., 2001).

The antimicrobial effects of PPy-g-CS and COP remain uncertain, but all the aforementioned events are expected to occur simultaneously and contribute to the antimicrobial activities with different intensities. In this context, PPy-g-CS outperformed COP to suppress the growth of E. coli. In general, the antimicrobial effectiveness of unmodified chitosan is stronger on Gram-negative than on Gram-positive strains (Da Silva et al., 2010; Jia & Xu, 2001). As mentioned earlier, the antimicrobial activity of the PPy-g-CS by chemical oxidative radical-polymerization is significantly enhanced compared to PPy and CS alone (Cabuk et al., 2015). Since a known amount of chitosan was used to synthesize the composite, the control solution was prepared with the calculated quantity of chitosan present in the grafted polymer. The antibacterial tests were performed at a concentration of 1 mg/mL of the grafted polymer, which included 0.250 mg/mL of chitosan. This is a substantially lower amount of chitosan compared to some literature reports. For comparison, the PPy decorated with silver nanomaterials exhibits antimicrobial and hemolysis activity (Ebrahimiasl et al., 2015; Zare et al., 2014). A nanocomposite of PPy/ZnO/CS was also used for antimicrobial action (Ebrahimiasl et al., 2015). A polyaniline-based nanomaterial with a minimum inhibition concentration of 3.5 mg/mL of polyaniline completely inhibits the E. coli growth. Generally, PPy and polyaniline, both are conducting polymers, are synthesized in acidic conditions and initiated by a strong oxidant such as ammonium persulfate, FeCl3, etc. Of importance is the presence of chitinases (glycosyl hydrolases) in several bacteria including E. coli (Hamid et al., 2013). Such enzymes might degrade chitin/chitosan to low molecular weight chitooligomers and this could be the reason why free chitosan is not effective against E. coli as described earlier. No proteins leak out of the cells or leaked proteins are too small detection when S. aureus is exposed to chitosan (Raafat et al., 2008), so this approach was not attempted in our study.

The plausible cytotoxicity of PPy-g-CS a brief comment here albeit this is a subject of future endeavors. As a biofunctional material, chitosan is well tolerated by living tissues and tested as a curative wound dressing and as scaffolds for tissue and bone engineering (Eaton, Fernandes, Pereira, Pintado, & Malcata, 2008; Fei Liu et al., 2001). It has also been advocated for use as edible coatings to prolong shelf-life and preserve the quality of fresh foods (Assis, 2004; Wilkerson, Copeland, Covington, & Trzaskos, 1995). Pyrrole and its derivatives are widely present in nature with diverse applications as fungicides (Goy et al., 2009), antibiotics (Goy et al., 2009), anti-inflammatory drugs (Wurz & Charette, 2005), cholesterol-reducing drugs (Wurz & Charette, 2005), antitumor agents (Lee et al., 2001; Mishra, Mittal, & Sharma, 2017; Mittal, Kumar, Mishra, Deva, & Sharma, 2016), etc. Therefore, PPy-g-CS should have negligible cytotoxicity at low concentrations. The cytotoxic effect is also dose as well as cell-dependent. As an example, PPy nanoparticles exhibit no cytotoxicity at concentrations below 9.7 μg/mL for three tested cell lines]: Jurkat cells, MH-22A and MEF cells (Vaitkuviene et al., 2013). At 19.4 μg/mL of PPy-NPs, no effect on cell behavior is observed for MH-22A and MEF cells whereas the proliferation of Jurkat cells is inhibited by 48% (Vaitkuviene et al., 2013). The chitosan moiety of PPy-g-CS can be subjected to a quaternization step to methylate the amino group to trimethyl chitosan, RN(CH3)3+ as mentioned earlier to enhance its antimicrobial activity. However, its resulting cytotoxicity might be affected and investigated. i.e., there is a balance between antimicrobial activity and cytotoxicity. The cationic groups of PPy-CS facilitate its electrostatic binding to bacterial cell surfaces with high net negative charge over human cells, i.e., this polymer should have more selective activity towards bacteria.

A facile one-step microwave-assisted procedure has been used for the syntheses of polypyrrole grafted chitosan and poly(pyrrole-N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine) (COP) with C-dots as the only polymerization initiators. The antimicrobial activities have been established for E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains using the simple CFU technique. Based on the experimental data, several combined mechanisms were postulated for cell lysis and death. The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged polymers and negatively charged microbial cells plays an important role in cell death. The two polymers chelate calcium and other nutrients and form an external barrier to suppress the penetration of essential nutrients to support microbial growth. Pyrrole grafted chitosan can be stacked onto the bacterial surface to impede the mass transfer and suppress the bacterial metabolic activity. The binding of chitosan to teichoic acids, important acids of Gram-positive bacteria would provoke a sequence of events, ultimately leading to bacterial death. All events occur simultaneously and contribute to the antimicrobial activities but at different intensities. The synthesis of this polymer is not costly or labor-intensive and its potential antimicrobial properties will warrant further development as a new class of antibacterial agents.

Moorthy Maruthapandi: Writing - original draft, Investigation. Kusha Sharma: Formal analysis. John H.T. Luong: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Aharon Gedanken: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Writing - review & editing.
